moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with 1.b3, a flexible first move that immediately supports our light-squared bishop on the long diagonal. This is the Nimzo-Larsen Attack, a hypermodern approach that avoids central commitments while preparing to fianchetto our bishop on b2—a powerful piece that will control the critical a1-h8 diagonal. You'll notice we're not fighting for the center immediately like in 1.e4 or 1.d4; instead, we're preparing to control it from afar with pieces, which gives us flexibility and can catch unprepared opponents off guard.

Black develops naturally with the knight, a solid and universal move that controls key central squares (e4 and d5) and prepares to support the center with moves like ...e6 or ...d6. This move doesn't commit to a specific pawn structure yet, keeping options open—Black might play an Indian setup, or prepare a central push depending on how we develop. Your opponent is wisely waiting to see whether you'll play a kingside fianchetto or commit to the center before deciding on their own setup.
